summary: The five radiographic densities from most to least opaque are metal, bone, soft tissue, fat, and air.
The five radiographic densities from most to least opaque are air, fat, soft tissue, bone, and metal.
There are only three radiographic densities: bone, soft tissue, and air.
Free gas under the diaphragm on an erect X-ray indicates perforation of a hollow viscus and is a surgical emergency.
Free gas under the diaphragm is a normal finding on erect abdominal X-ray.
Free gas under the diaphragm indicates bowel obstruction rather than perforation.
Barium sulfate is contraindicated when GI perforation is suspected because it causes severe chemical peritonitis.
Barium sulfate is safe to use even when perforation is suspected.
Water-soluble contrast is contraindicated in suspected perforation.
CT uses Hounsfield units where water is 0, bone is +400 to +1000, and air is -1000.
In CT, air measures 0 HU and water measures -1000 HU.
CT Hounsfield units range from 0 to 100 only.
Ultrasound uses high-frequency sound waves and is the first-line imaging for pregnancy and biliary disease.
Ultrasound uses ionising radiation similar to X-rays.
Ultrasound cannot differentiate between solid and cystic masses.
MRI uses magnetic fields and radiofrequency pulses and is best for soft tissue contrast, especially in the brain and spine.
MRI uses X-rays to produce cross-sectional images.
MRI is the first-line investigation for acute trauma and bony fractures.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Revise contrast agents and Hounsfield units.
